message.content:
Repo: /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay (branch harden/pass-1). Venv: /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/.venv/bin/python.
/tmp/pass1_reconfirm.json is a JSON array of 10 pass-1 findings that were strengthened after a first confirmation pass found them 'appropriate but incomplete'. Each item has: issue, title, file, problem, fix_update (what was just added/changed).
Constraints (NOT bugs): local-first/data-sovereign, no metered API, engine imports no adapter, lazy optional backends.

You are independent CONFIRMER #2 of 2 for INDEX 2 (0-based) in /tmp/pass1_reconfirm.json.
Read that item (issue, problem, fix_update). Then verify the CURRENT state on branch harden/pass-1: read the changed source AND the named regression test(s), and run a PoC / the specific tests against the venv where useful. Decide: appropriate = the fix soundly addresses the root cause (no hack/over-claim); complete = it fully resolves the finding AND has a regression test that would actually catch a regression (a test that passes equally with/without the fix does NOT count). If the item documents a deliberately-deferred scope (e.g. #119's Protocol-boundary note), judge completeness against that honestly-scoped fix, not an unbounded ideal. Set either false with specifics if not met. Echo the issue number.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

This reproduces both halves of the codebase's own documented issue #7 (see src/assay_engine/_frozen.py module docstring):\n\n1. Mutable-",
  "fix_update": "GateReview.__post_init__ freezes payload; NEW test test_gate_review_payload_is_frozen_and_hashable asserts FrozenDict + mutation raises + hashable."
}

98	def auto_approve(review: GateReview) -> GateDecision:
99	    """A non-interactive gate handler: approve and record the decision.
100	
101	    This is the explicit "no operator" choice — it is **not** the default, so a study cannot
102	    silently bypass human review by omitting a handler (it must consciously pass this). Replace
103	    with an operator-driven handler (e.g. one wired to the LangGraph gate interrupt/resume in
104	    :mod:`assay_engine.orchestration`) for genuine human-in-the-loop parking; the handler may
105	    return ``approved=False`` to block the transition.
106	    """
107	    return GateDecision(
108	        approved=True,
109	        gate=review.gate,
110	        reason="non-interactive auto-approval",
111	        evidence={"transition": f"{review.frm.name}->{review.to.name}"},
112	    )

451	def test_gate_review_payload_is_frozen_and_hashable(tmp_path):

454	    from assay_engine.pipeline import GateReview
455	
456	    r = GateReview(
457	        gate="g",

464	    with pytest.raises(Exception):  # noqa: B017 — any mutation must fail
465	        r.payload["a"] = 2  # type: ignore[index]
466	    hash(r)  # frozen record is hashable

content.text: Now let me run the test, and verify it actually fails without the fix.
Now let me confirm the test would actually catch a regression by temporarily reverting the fix.

cd /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay && .venv/bin/python -c "
import re
src = open('src/assay_engine/pipeline.py').read()
# simulate removing the __post_init__ for GateReview only
broken = src.replace(
'''    def __post_init__(self) -> None:
        # deep-freeze the payload so this frozen record is truly immutable + hashable (#113)
        object.__setattr__(self, \"payload\", freeze_mapping(self.payload))


GateHandler''',
'''
GateHandler''')
assert broken != src, 'replace failed'
import types, sys
mod = types.ModuleType('pipeline_broken')
# Build a minimal reproduction instead: define a frozen dataclass without freeze
from dataclasses import dataclass
from typing import Mapping, Any
import enum
class Phase(enum.Enum):
    PREREGISTER=1; CONFIRM=2
@dataclass(frozen=True, slots=True)
class GR:
    payload: Mapping[str, Any]
from assay_engine._frozen import FrozenDict
r = GR(payload={'a':{'b':1}})
print('is FrozenDict (should be False without fix):', isinstance(r.payload, FrozenDict))
try:
    r.payload['a']=2
    print('mutation succeeded (no fix): TEST WOULD FAIL? mutation did NOT raise')
except Exception as e:
    print('mutation raised:', type(e).__name__)
try:
    hash(r)
    print('hash succeeded')
except Exception as e:
    print('hash raised:', type(e).__name__)
"
input.description: Verify test discriminates without the fix

content.content:
is FrozenDict (should be False without fix): False
mutation succeeded (no fix): TEST WOULD FAIL? mutation did NOT raise
hash raised: TypeError

content.text:
Confirmed. Without the fix: payload is a plain dict (not FrozenDict, so `assert isinstance(r.payload, FrozenDict)` fails), mutation succeeds (so `pytest.raises` fails), and `hash(r)` raises TypeError. All three assertions in the test discriminate. The test genuinely catches a regression.

The fix mirrors the exact pattern used by every other frozen mapping-bearing record in the engine (`object.__setattr__` + `freeze_mapping` in `__post_init__`), addressing the documented issue #7 root cause. No hack, no over-claim.

input.reasoning:
Issue #113: GateReview was a frozen dataclass with a `payload: Mapping[str, Any]` field but, unlike every other frozen mapping-bearing record in the engine, lacked a __post_init__ that normalized the payload through freeze_mapping — reproducing both halves of the codebase's documented issue #7 (mutable-in-place dict + unhashable record).

CURRENT STATE on harden/pass-1 is the soundly-fixed version:
- src/assay_engine/pipeline.py:90-92 adds `def __post_init__` that calls `object.__setattr__(self, "payload", freeze_mapping(self.payload))`. This is the identical, idiomatic pattern used across the engine (the same approach ProvenanceEntry/GateDecision/etc. use), so it addresses the root cause directly with no hack and no over-claim. freeze_mapping returns a FrozenDict whose leaves are deep-frozen and which has a real __hash__, fixing both the mutability and hashability halves.

APPROPRIATE: yes. The fix is the canonical engine pattern, not a workaround.

COMPLETE: yes, with a discriminating regression test. test_gate_review_payload_is_frozen_and_hashable (tests/test_pipeline.py:451) constructs a GateReview with a nested dict + list payload and asserts (1) isinstance(payload, FrozenDict), (2) mutation `r.payload["a"]=2` raises, (3) hash(r) succeeds. I ran it against the venv: PASSES. I then verified the test actually catches a regression by reproducing the pre-fix state (frozen dataclass with a raw dict payload): isinstance(FrozenDict) is False (assert fails), mutation succeeds (pytest.raises fails), and hash(r) raises TypeError. All three assertions flip without the fix, so the test is not a no-op — it would genuinely catch a regression.
input.evidence: src/assay_engine/pipeline.py:80-92 (GateReview frozen dataclass + __post_init__ calling freeze_mapping); import at pipeline.py:38. Regression test at tests/test_pipeline.py:451-466. freeze_mapping/FrozenDict at src/assay_engine/_frozen.py:24,50,105 (FrozenDict.__hash__ + deep freeze). Ran `.venv/bin/python -m pytest tests/test_pipeline.py::test_gate_review_payload_is_frozen_and_hashable -q` -> 1 passed. Pre-fix reproduction showed isinstance(FrozenDict)=False, mutation did NOT raise, hash raised TypeError — confirming the test discriminates.

content.text: Confirmed issue #113 as both appropriate and complete.
